lesson_title: Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: The sign challenge: array sizes are {{c1::unsigned}} (std::size_t), but {{c2::signed}} integers are preferred for loop counters, creating {{c3::comparison and conversion}} issues.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: To avoid sign conversion warnings when using signed loop counters, use {{c1::std::ssize()}} instead of {{c2::.size()}} to get the vector length as a signed value.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.concept: Solutions to the sign challenge with arrays
cards.explanation:
Three main approaches:
  • Use std::ssize() (C++20) to get signed size
  • Use static_cast<int>(vec.size()) for older C++
  • Use std::size_t for counters (not recommended - wraparound issues)
LearnCPP recommends using signed integers with std::ssize().
examples: for (std::size_t i{0}; i < vec.size(); ++i)  // Not recommended
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.question: What warning might this code generate?
cards.explanation: i is signed int, vec.size() is unsigned. Comparing them triggers a warning. Use std::ssize(vec) instead.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: To convert an unsigned size to signed int in pre-C++20: {{c1::static_cast<int>(vec.size())}}
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.front: What problem occurs when you use unsigned std::size_t for a loop that decrements to 0?
cards.back: When the counter reaches 0 and decrements, it wraps around to the maximum unsigned value (like 18446744073709551615), causing an infinite loop.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.question: Which version is better and why?
cards.answer: Version B - uses signed int which is safer and more intuitive
cards.explanation: Signed integers avoid wraparound issues, work with negative values, and are more natural for counters and indices.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: The std::ssize() function was introduced in {{c1::C++20}}.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: When comparing signed and unsigned integers, the {{c1::signed}} value is implicitly converted to {{c2::unsigned}}, which can cause unexpected results if the signed value is {{c3::negative}}.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions

cards.text: The recommended type for array indices and loop counters is {{c1::signed}} integers like {{c2::int}} or std::ptrdiff_t. Use {{c3::std::ssize()}} to get container sizes as signed.
cards.source: 16.7 - Arrays, loops, and sign challenge solutions
